meta.name: Severe TBI with Herniation — Neuroprotective RSI

patient.name: Tyler Brennan
patient.chiefComplaint: Unresponsive after approximately 20-foot fall from rock climbing wall

history.hpi: 28-year-old male outdoor climber found unresponsive at the base of a bolted sport route by his climbing partner. Estimated 20-foot ground fall. Partner states he was belaying when the anchor failed. No helmet.
history.lastOralIntake: Gatorade and granola bar approximately 2 hours prior
history.events: Lead climber took an uncontrolled ground fall from approximately 20 feet after anchor failure. Brief seizure-like activity witnessed immediately after impact per partner. Vomited once. Has not regained consciousness.

scene.dispatch: Medic 7, respond to Eagle Bluff Crag, trail access via Ridge Road, for a 28-year-old male, fall victim, unresponsive. Wilderness rescue team en route.
scene.location: Outdoor climbing area, base of a cliff on packed dirt and small boulders. Approximately 200 yards from the road via a mild uphill trail. Patient at base of route with climbing rope and hardware around him.
scene.safety: Scene cleared by partner — no further rockfall risk. Uneven terrain for working area. Rescue rope team will assist with litter carry-out.
scene.bystanders: Climbing partner present, witnessed the fall, provides mechanism and timeline. Two other climbers assisting with scene access.
visualCues: Young adult male supine on the dirt, unresponsive
Obvious large left parietal scalp hematoma with oozing
Bilateral arm flexion toward chest (decorticate posturing) with painful stimuli
Vomitus around mouth and on left cheek
Loud sonorous respirations audible from several feet away
Climbing helmet is NOT on patient — still clipped to harness

phases.name: Arrival — Severe TBI with Herniation Signs
phases.description: Crew arrives to find patient with obvious severe head injury. Unequal pupils and decorticate posturing signal impending herniation. Sonorous airway with vomitus pooling. Spinal precautions required. Crew must recognize this as a neuroprotective RSI case and manage aggressively to prevent secondary brain injury.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Sonorous respirations with vomitus pooling in oropharynx; airway patent only with jaw thrust
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Irregular, deep respirations (Cushing response pattern) at 10/min; symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Bounding radial pulse; widened pulse pressure
clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT pupil 6 mm, fixed and non-reactive; RIGHT pupil 3 mm, sluggishly reactive
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Decorticate posturing (bilateral arm flexion) to painful stimulus; lower extremities extend; M3 on GCS motor
otherFindings: Large left parietal boggy hematoma with overlying laceration
Left forearm deformity suggestive of radius/ulna fracture
Cushing triad: HR 52, BP 188/110, irregular respirations

expectedActions.action: Manual cervical stabilization with in-line immobilization throughout airway management
Aggressive oropharyngeal suctioning with Yankauer to clear vomitus
Jaw thrust airway maneuver (no head tilt) with OPA placement
2-person BVM at 10 breaths/min with PEEP 5 cmH2O and high-flow oxygen; target SpO2 ≥94% and EtCO2 35-45 mmHg
expectedActions.rationale: Avoid both hypoxemia and hyperventilation — both worsen secondary brain injury.

expectedActions.action: Do NOT place nasopharyngeal airway given risk of basilar skull fracture
Elevate head of spine board 30 degrees (reverse Trendelenburg) to reduce ICP
Apply monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography, frequent BP (every 3 minutes)
Establish two large-bore IVs; draw blood for destination hospital
Check blood glucose to rule out alternate cause of AMS

phases.name: Decision and Preparation for Neuroprotective RSI
phases.description: Despite OPA and BVM, airway remains compromised by recurrent vomitus and the patient cannot protect his own airway. SpO2 is borderline. Crew prepares for neuroprotective RSI, verbalizing the plan, optimizing hemodynamics, and organizing the team for a controlled intubation.
phases.triggerCondition: Persistent airway compromise with GCS ≤8 and signs of herniation

transitions.condition: RSI performed with confirmed tube placement and SBP maintained
Significant hyperventilation or hypotension during intubation

clinicalPresentation.airway: OPA in place, jaw thrust maintained, recurrent emesis suctioned
clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT pupil remains 6 mm fixed; RIGHT 4 mm sluggish
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: M2 (extension) — motor score has worsened from M3 indicating progression
otherFindings: Progressive motor deterioration (M3 → M2) indicates continued herniation

expectedActions.action: Verbalize structured RSI plan including roles, equipment check, primary and backup airway strategies, and hemodynamic targets
Preoxygenate with 2-person BVM + PEEP 5 cmH2O for at least 3 minutes; apply apneic oxygenation via NC at 15 LPM
Maintain SBP ≥110 mmHg with normal saline bolus as needed; check BP immediately prior to induction (hypertension currently protective)
Administer Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg (80 mcg) IV push 2-3 minutes prior to intubation to blunt ICP response to laryngoscopy
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (120 mg for 80 kg patient) as induction agent; ketamine preserves hemodynamics and is appropriate in TBI
expectedActions.rationale: Contemporary evidence supports ketamine in TBI — its presumed ICP elevation is outweighed by hemodynamic stability. Etomidate 0.2-0.3 mg/kg (16-24 mg) is also acceptable.

expectedActions.action: Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (80 mg for 80 kg patient) as paralytic
Perform endotracheal intubation with video laryngoscopy while maintaining in-line cervical stabilization; 8.0 mm cuffed ETT; limit attempt to 15 seconds or SpO2 <90%
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds; secure at the teeth

phases.name: Post-Intubation Neuroprotective Management
phases.description: Tube confirmed. Patient paralyzed and sedated. Crew must ventilate precisely to avoid both hypoventilation (worsened ICP from hypercapnia) and hyperventilation (cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia). Target EtCO2 40 mmHg, SBP ≥110, head of bed elevated, emergent transport to a trauma center.
phases.triggerCondition: Successful RSI with confirmed tube placement

clinicalPresentation.airway: ETT in place, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated at 10 breaths/min, symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.pupils: LEFT 6 mm fixed, RIGHT 4 mm sluggish — unchanged; indicates ongoing herniation
otherFindings: Head of spine board elevated 30 degrees
Cushing response softening — HR climbing, BP slightly decreasing after induction

expectedActions.action: Ventilate at 10 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight, titrated to EtCO2 35-45 mmHg (target 40); do NOT hyperventilate despite herniation signs
expectedActions.rationale: Prior guidance recommended hyperventilation for herniation — current evidence shows hyperventilation worsens outcome via cerebral vasoconstriction. Maintain normocapnia.

expectedActions.action: Post-intubation sedation: Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg (1.6 mg) IV AND Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg (80-160 mcg) IV; titrate to RASS -3 to -4
Maintain SBP ≥110 mmHg — bolus NS 500 mL if SBP trends toward threshold
Maintain 30-degree head-of-bed elevation (reverse Trendelenburg on spine board)
Expedited transport to Level I trauma center; early trauma activation and neurosurgical notification
Serial pupil and motor reassessment every 5 minutes; document any changes
Splint left forearm fracture during transport if time permits — low priority compared to airway and brain

phases.name: Branch: Secondary Injury from Hyperventilation or Hypotension
phases.description: Crew inadvertently hyperventilates the patient (respiratory rate 20+, EtCO2 drops below 30) and/or allows transient hypotension during induction. Cerebral perfusion suffers. The patient progresses toward complete herniation.
phases.triggerCondition: Hyperventilation (EtCO2 <30) OR SBP drops <110 during or after induction

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Hypotensive or normotensive depending on precipitant
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Both pupils now 6 mm, fixed and dilated bilaterally
otherFindings: Bilateral fixed dilated pupils indicate progression to full herniation
Cushing response has collapsed — HR rising, BP falling (terminal pattern)

expectedActions.action: Slow ventilation rate to 10/min and adjust tidal volume to return EtCO2 to 40 mmHg
Aggressive fluid resuscitation with NS to restore SBP ≥110 mmHg
Recognize that bilateral fixed pupils with loss of Cushing response indicates severe progression and expedite transport

criticalActions: Maintain cervical spine immobilization throughout airway management
Jaw thrust with OPA (no NPA due to basilar skull fracture risk)
2-person BVM with PEEP at 10 breaths/min, target SpO2 ≥94% and EtCO2 35-45
Do NOT hyperventilate despite herniation signs
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (120 mg) + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg (80 mg)
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Post-intubation sedation with Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg
Transport to Level I trauma center with early activation
expectedActions: Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg premedication to blunt ICP response
Apneic oxygenation via NC 15 LPM during RSI
bonusActions: Recognize Cushing triad and its clinical significance
Articulate why hyperventilation worsens TBI outcomes despite intuition to lower ICP
Prepare fluids pre-induction to prevent post-sedative hypotension

learningObjectives: Recognize signs of impending cerebral herniation (unilateral blown pupil, posturing, Cushing triad)
Apply the MATC Head Injury protocol to prevent secondary brain injury
Execute neuroprotective RSI with attention to hemodynamics and ventilation targets
Understand why hyperventilation is contraindicated in TBI despite herniation signs
Coordinate airway management with spinal precautions
discussionQuestions: What are the three components of the Cushing response and what do they tell you?
Why is hyperventilation harmful in TBI when it seems like it would lower ICP?
How would your induction agent choice change if this patient were hypotensive?
What is the rationale for avoiding NPAs in patients with potential basilar skull fractures?
How do you balance transport time against thorough scene care in a TBI patient?
commonPitfalls: Allowing peri-intubation hypotension which devastates cerebral perfusion
Placing an NPA in a patient with basilar skull fracture findings
Under-sedating post-intubation because the patient 'looks' comfortable on paralytic
keyTakeaways: The two deadliest enemies in TBI are hypoxia and hypotension — prevent both
Normocapnia (EtCO2 35-45) is the target; hyperventilation causes cerebral ischemia
Cerebral perfusion pressure depends on MAP — keep SBP ≥110
Paralytic provides a calm intubation but does nothing for the underlying brain injury
